rationale: Traced the guard's own prescribed remediation through the real code path (independently of other reviewers, then cross-checked): checkOpenQuestions (internal/validate/spec.go:226-248) errors on any '- [ ]' line in Open Questions, and ApproveSpec (internal/approve/spec.go:47-50) hard-fails `mindspec spec approve` on it, no bypass. The guard's fix for headless sessions is to append an unchecked '- [ ] grill deferred...' line there. Worse: because the grill is now SKIPPED (not run-with-defaults) in headless mode, nothing resolves the spec template's own pre-existing '- [ ] <Question that must be resolved before planning>' placeholder either — previously the grill's own 'resolve or explicitly defer every Open Question' contract cleared it (PR #155's workaround kept the grill running-with-defaults, which is why TestLLM_SpecToIdle passed). So a fully headless spec-create-through-approve run now hits `mindspec spec approve` carrying 1-2 unresolved Open Questions and hard-fails — directly contradicting the bead's own stated goal ('auto-defer the grill with a recorded default, rather than blocking'). Marker durability/auditability (job 3) IS real and good (verified byte-identical across 6 files, verified the gate is non-bypassable) — but that same mechanism is what now blocks the exact autopilot-blocking scenario mindspec-0uur exists to fix, untested and undocumented. Also found a real, if softer, over-trigger wording risk.
concrete_changes_required: Reconcile the deferral marker with the Open-Questions hard gate before this can be called fixed for the bead's own stated goal. Right now the bead text says the headless path should 'auto-defer the grill with a recorded default, rather than blocking' — but the shipped guard still blocks, just later (at `mindspec spec approve` instead of at the Q&A prompt), and via an undocumented CLI failure that no skill (ms-spec-approve, ms-spec-autopilot, spec-orchestrator) tells the agent how to recover from. Pick one horn deliberately: (a) make the deferral genuinely non-blocking — e.g. write the deferral as prose starting with 'None' (checkOpenQuestions' only escape hatch, internal/validate/spec.go:237) plus an explanatory note instead of an unchecked checkbox, and separately ensure the headless path also clears/replaces the template's own default placeholder line so it doesn't independently trip the same gate — while keeping the deferral discoverable some other way (e.g. `mindspec validate spec` WARNING, not ERROR); or (b) keep it blocking on purpose, and then explicitly teach the downstream skills (ms-spec-approve step 3's 'help the user fix them', ms-spec-autopilot, spec-orchestrator) how to auto-resolve this specific marker and retry approve in a fully headless run. Either way, add a test — unit-level (validate.ValidateSpec / approve.ApproveSpec against a spec.md carrying exactly the guard's marker) or a harness re-run of the ScenarioSpecToIdle-style fully-headless lifecycle — that pins the chosen end-to-end behavior. The two new tests in this diff (TestInstallSkills_RefreshesPreHeadlessGuardSnapshot/...GrillSnapshot) only cover skill-file refresh, not this interaction.

Tighten the over-trigger surface in 'invoked by an orchestrator' (both guard copies: plugins/mindspec/skills/ms-spec-grill/SKILL.md and the ms-spec-create clause in internal/setup/claude.go + mirrors). This repo's own convention (CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md/user memory) uses 'orchestrator' to describe the TOP-LEVEL INTERACTIVE session a human drives turn-by-turn ('I decide + run gates, not hold the pen') — the same kind of session that runs `/ms-spec-create` directly. A model primed by that self-referential vocabulary could misread 'invoked by an orchestrator' as matching its own role even while a human is actively present and answering. The primary test ('no human available to answer one-at-a-time') is sound and should dominate, but the parenthetical example is the weak link — make explicit that this means a dispatch with no back-channel to a human (a Task-tool/subagent invocation, or an unattended `ms-spec-autopilot`/CI run), not a human-directed session that happens to follow orchestrator-style delegation conventions.

findings.area: approve-gate collision vs. bead's own stated goal
findings.detail: internal/validate/spec.go checkOpenQuestions (226-248) flags every unresolved '- [ ]' line in Open Questions as a SevError; internal/approve/spec.go ApproveSpec (47-50) returns a hard error when vr.HasFailures(), with no --force/bypass (confirmed in cmd/mindspec/spec.go: approveSpecRunE has no skip-validation flag). The guard's prescribed remediation is exactly a '- [ ] grill deferred...' line in that section. Under the OLD PR #155 workaround the grill still ran (with 'make reasonable default decisions'), so its own 'resolve or explicitly defer every Open Question' contract cleared the template's default placeholder ('- [ ] <Question that must be resolved before planning>', internal/spec/create.go:67) before `mindspec spec approve` was ever called — that is why TestLLM_SpecToIdle passed. Under the NEW guard the grill is skipped entirely, so neither the template placeholder nor the new marker gets resolved, and a fully headless spec-create->approve pass (the ScenarioSpecToIdle prompt literally says 'This is an automated, non-interactive run: there is NO human available to answer questions... make reasonable default decisions and proceed', internal/harness/scenario_spec_lifecycle.go) now hits a hard `mindspec spec approve` failure that nothing in this diff exercises or documents a recovery path for. Bead mindspec-0uur's own description says the fix should 'auto-defer the grill with a recorded default, rather than blocking' — as shipped it still blocks, just downstream and silently (from the fix's perspective; no test catches it).

Independently verified: the spec template (internal/spec/create.go specTemplate) always ships an '## Open Questions' section, so the guard's instruction to append there always has a real target. checkOpenQuestions + ApproveSpec together make the deferral marker genuinely non-silent — a spec CANNOT reach approval with the grill deferred and nobody noticing; the CLI errors out and ms-spec-approve/SKILL.md step 3 tells the agent to surface the validation error. This directly answers the 'can it sail to approval silently' question: no. The gap (finding above) is not silence, it's that the resulting hard-failure is the wrong shape for the bead's 'don't block' goal and is unhandled by any downstream skill.

Both guard copies list 'invoked by/running under an orchestrator' as an illustrative headless signal. This repo's own AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md/memory conventions use 'orchestrator' for the human-driven top-level interactive session itself (which runs gate commands like /ms-spec-create directly, per user memory 'Orchestrator delegates authoring... I decide + run gates, not hold the pen'). The guard's primary/operative test ('no human available to answer one-at-a-time') is well-formed and should correctly override surface keyword matching in a capable model, so this is a real but lower-probability risk — not a proven bug, a wording weak spot given this specific repo's vocabulary.

For genuine Task-tool/subagent dispatches and `claude -p` headless invocations, there is a structural signal separate from the guard's wording: those sessions architecturally cannot receive interim human replies (single prompt in, single report out), which models can generally recognize on their own — this lowers under-trigger risk for the harness/CI/subagent cases the guard names. The weaker case is the TOP-LEVEL interactive session running `ms-spec-autopilot` unattended (e.g. overnight autonomous runs per project convention) — there is no structural cue distinguishing 'actively watched' from 'left running', so correctness there depends entirely on the model recognizing it is executing under the named `ms-spec-autopilot` skill context, which the guard's explicit naming of 'ms-spec-autopilot' does support reasonably well.

Byte-compared the marker string across all 6 locations (plugins/mindspec/skills/ms-spec-grill/SKILL.md, .claude/skills/ms-spec-grill/SKILL.md, .agents/skills/ms-spec-grill/SKILL.md, .claude/skills/ms-spec-create/SKILL.md, .agents/skills/ms-spec-create/SKILL.md, internal/setup/claude.go's Go-string-concatenated literal) via a script that extracted and diffed each: '- [ ] grill deferred: headless session — run /ms-spec-grill interactively before approval.' is byte-identical in all 6, including the em-dash. A drifted marker would silently break grep-based tooling; there is none here. Trigger-criteria wording between the two guard copies differs only in verb choice appropriate to each skill's control flow ('return immediately, skipping the rest of this skill' in ms-spec-grill vs. 'do NOT enter the grill loop... proceed' in ms-spec-create's step 5) — not drift.

Both guard copies name 'ms-spec-autopilot' as a headless-trigger example, but .claude/skills/ms-spec-autopilot/SKILL.md's own loop never calls ms-spec-create — it explicitly says it takes 'a mindspec-approved plan' and loops /ms-bead-cycle, starting after spec approval. The realistic caller that reaches ms-spec-create headlessly is more likely the `spec-orchestrator` agent type (whose description starts 'spec -> plan -> bead -> PR -> merge') or a bare harness/CI invocation. Not wrong, just an imprecise example — low priority, does not block.
